meta.title: 3.5 Indexing Strategies
meta.description: B-tree vs LSM-tree, secondary indexes, composite indexes, covering indexes, and full-text search.

blocks.prompt: A database has 100 million rows. A query without an index takes 30 seconds. With the right index, it takes 5ms. How can an index make a query 6,000x faster? What data structure makes this possible?

blocks.content:
## B-tree vs LSM-tree

The two fundamental index structures used by modern databases.

### B-tree (PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB, SQL Server)

A balanced tree where each node contains sorted keys and pointers. Reads are fast (O(log n)). Writes update the tree in-place.

**Characteristics:**
- Read-optimized: lookups, range scans, and ordering are fast
- Write amplification: each write may update multiple pages
- Best for: read-heavy workloads, OLTP with balanced read/write

### LSM-tree (Cassandra, RocksDB, LevelDB, HBase)

Writes go to an in-memory buffer (memtable). When full, flushed to sorted files on disk (SSTables). Background compaction merges files.

**Characteristics:**
- Write-optimized: sequential writes are fast
- Read amplification: may need to check multiple SSTables
- Best for: write-heavy workloads, time-series, logging

| Feature | B-tree | LSM-tree |
|---|---|---|
| Read speed | Fast | Moderate (check multiple levels) |
| Write speed | Moderate (random I/O) | Fast (sequential I/O) |
| Space efficiency | Moderate (page fragmentation) | Good (compaction removes duplicates) |
| Write amplification | Moderate | Higher (compaction rewrites) |
| Use case | OLTP, read-heavy | Write-heavy, time-series |

## Secondary indexes

The primary index is on the primary key. Secondary indexes enable fast lookups on other columns.

**Example:** Users table with primary key `user_id`. Secondary index on `email` enables `WHERE email = 'user@example.com'` without scanning all rows.

**Cost:** Every write must update all secondary indexes. More indexes = slower writes.

**Rule of thumb:** Index columns you query frequently. Do not index columns you rarely filter on. Monitor slow query logs to find missing indexes.

## Advanced indexing

**Partial indexes:** Index only rows matching a condition. `CREATE INDEX ON orders(created_at) WHERE status = 'pending'` — smaller, faster, focused.

**Expression indexes:** Index computed values. `CREATE INDEX ON users(LOWER(email))` — enables case-insensitive lookups.

**Full-text search indexes:** Inverted indexes that map words to documents. PostgreSQL `tsvector`/`tsquery`, or dedicated search engines (Elasticsearch).

blocks.question: You are designing a logging system that ingests 100,000 events per second. Queries are relatively infrequent — mainly searching recent logs by timestamp. Should you use a B-tree or LSM-tree based storage engine? Why?
blocks.answer: **LSM-tree.** The workload is heavily write-dominated (100K writes/sec vs infrequent reads). LSM-trees excel here because writes go to an in-memory buffer and are flushed as sequential disk writes — much faster than B-tree's random I/O page updates. The read amplification cost of LSM-trees (checking multiple SSTables) is acceptable because reads are infrequent. Additionally, time-series data is naturally sorted by timestamp, which means SSTables are already partially ordered, making range queries on recent data efficient. This is exactly why Cassandra (LSM-tree) is popular for logging and time-series workloads.

blocks.content: B-trees optimize for reads (balanced tree, O(log n) lookups); LSM-trees optimize for writes (sequential I/O, buffered writes). Choose based on your read/write ratio. Secondary indexes speed up queries but slow down writes. Index what you query; do not over-index.

blocks.prompt: Indexes make individual queries fast. Module 3.6 covers data modeling for scale — how to design your schemas so that your most common queries are efficient by default.

reviewQuestions.question: Which index type is optimized for write-heavy workloads?
reviewQuestions.explanation: LSM-trees buffer writes in memory and flush as sequential disk I/O, much faster for writes.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Right! LSM-trees are the go-to for write-heavy workloads like logging.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: LSM-trees are optimized for writes — they buffer in memory and flush sequentially to disk.

reviewQuestions.question: What is the read/write tradeoff of adding database indexes?
options: Indexes speed up both reads and writes
Indexes speed up reads but slow down writes
Indexes only help with full table scans
reviewQuestions.explanation: Indexes speed up queries but slow down writes because every insert/update must also update the index.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Exactly! Every index you add costs write performance. Index what you query.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Indexes speed up reads but slow down writes — every write must also maintain the index.
